compiled_state.title: [Review orchestrator]: reviewer dispatch uses general-purpose subagent -> bypasses named dso:code-reviewer-* agent and classifier-based tier selection
compiled_state.author: Test
compiled_state.description:
## Expected Behavior

When a skill orchestrator runs /dso:review (or executes REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md inline), it should:
1. Run `review-complexity-classifier.sh` to select the tier (light/standard/deep).
2. Dispatch the named tier agent directly via `Agent` with `subagent_type: "dso:code-reviewer-light"` (or `dso:code-reviewer-standard`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-*`), which are registered as plugin sub-agents in the session agent list.

Memory guidance reinforces this:
- feedback_named_review_agents.md: "Load named dso:code-reviewer-* agent file verbatim for every review — never construct prompts or use generic sub-agents for reviews."
- feedback_completion_verifier_must_use_named_agent.md: "Phase 6 validation MUST use dso:completion-verifier named agent; general-purpose substitution is prohibited." (same anti-pattern for a different named agent.)

## Actual Behavior

In this session (bug aa29-5cdc fix + bb42-1291 fix), the orchestrator:
1. DID run the classifier — selected tier=standard correctly.
2. Then dispatched `subagent_type: "general-purpose"` with the contents of `agents/code-reviewer-standard.md` pasted into the prompt, instead of using `subagent_type: "dso:code-reviewer-standard"`.

This bypasses the named-agent registration and turns the review into an ad-hoc prompted general-purpose agent run.

## Orchestrator's rationalization (verbatim reasoning, for diagnosis)

`REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md` Step 4 states at lines 209-214:
> **Inline dispatch is mandatory — `dso:*` labels are agent file identifiers, NOT `subagent_type` values.** The Agent tool only accepts built-in types (`general-purpose`, `Explore`, `Plan`, etc.). For every dispatch below:
> 1. Read `agents/<agent-name>.md` inline (strip the `dso:` prefix to get the file name).
> 2. Use `subagent_type: "general-purpose"` and the `model:` value from the agent file's frontmatter.
> 3. Pass the agent file content verbatim as the prompt, appending only the per-review context items listed below.

I followed this instruction literally: treated `dso:code-reviewer-*` as non-dispatchable labels, loaded the agent file content inline, and dispatched general-purpose with the content as the prompt.

**This rationalization was wrong.** The session's available-agent list (system prompt at top of every turn) explicitly enumerates `dso:code-reviewer-light`, `dso:code-reviewer-standard`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-arch`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-correctness`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-hygiene`, `dso:code-reviewer-deep-verification`, `dso:code-reviewer-performance`, `dso:code-reviewer-security-red-team`, `dso:code-reviewer-security-blue-team`, and `dso:code-reviewer-test-quality` as subagent_type values. The REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md claim that "Agent tool only accepts built-in types" is stale — named plugin agents ARE dispatchable via the Agent tool's subagent_type parameter.

## Impact

1. Reviews bypass the built-in classifier→named-agent binding that the plugin tier-review system is designed around (named agents have tier-tuned prompts, model constraints, and output contracts registered in plugin.json).
2. The same-shape rationalization applies to other named-agent dispatches (completion-verifier, intent-search, bot-psychologist, etc.) — all at risk of being silently downgraded to general-purpose if their dispatching workflow instructs "general-purpose with agent file content verbatim."
3. Prior memory feedback (feedback_named_review_agents, feedback_completion_verifier_must_use_named_agent) flagged the same anti-pattern in previous sessions. Recurrence suggests the fix for those incidents was corrective (note in memory) rather than source-level (fix the workflow doc instructing the behavior).

## Reproduction

1. Begin a session with the DSO plugin enabled.
2. Check the agent list in the opening system prompt — confirm `dso:code-reviewer-standard` is listed as an available subagent_type.
3. Invoke /dso:review (or execute REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md inline from within another skill).
4. Observe that the Step 4 dispatch uses `subagent_type: "general-purpose"` with the agent file pasted into the prompt, rather than `subagent_type: "dso:code-reviewer-standard"`.

## Investigation starting points

- REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 4 lines 207–225 (the instruction to use general-purpose). This text should either be removed (if named agents ARE dispatchable — confirmed in this session) or reconciled with environments where they aren't.
- Check whether `/dso:review` skill and REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md were updated after named agents were registered, or whether they still reflect the pre-registration design.
- Look for other workflows instructing "dispatch as general-purpose with agent file content verbatim" — same failure class.
- feedback_named_review_agents.md memory entry notes this should be corrected; check whether any source change landed to enforce it beyond memory-note status.

comments.body: Step 1: Classification: behavioral, Score: 1 (BASIC). Single dispatch site issue in REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md Step 4 — stale instruction to use general-purpose instead of named dso:code-reviewer-* subagent_type. Gate 1a: skipped — CLI_user tag present; intent-aligned assumed.
comments.author: Test

comments.body:
Investigation RESULT (BASIC tier):

ROOT_CAUSE: code-review-dispatch.md is a legacy template with misleading language ('Only general-purpose sub-agents have the Bash tool') that was never accurate for the named-agent verbatim dispatch pattern — named agents ARE dispatched as general-purpose (that IS the mechanism), so the Bash tool is available either way; retaining this file creates a false rationalization to use a generic fabricated prompt instead of loading named agent files verbatim.

IMPORTANT CLARIFICATION: The ticket's 'expected behavior' (use subagent_type: 'dso:code-reviewer-standard' directly) is INCORRECT. general-purpose + verbatim agent file content IS the designed pattern per REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md lines 209-212. The actual defect is code-review-dispatch.md itself.

No callers of code-review-dispatch.md found (the 'code-review-dispatch' identifier in validate-review-output.sh is an independent schema validator, unrelated to the .md file).

Two competing fixes proposed — requires user approval.
comments.author: Test
